title: Manage payments - Financial claims | CanadaBuys

breadcrumbs.title: Claims for progress payments and invoicing
Administrative guidance for implementing contract amendments
Suspension of the work and stop work orders
Termination of contracts with financial security
Public Services and Procurement Canada offices outside Canada

structure_blocks.heading: Financial claims
structure_blocks.text: Financial claims

structure_blocks.heading: Financial claims
structure_blocks.text: This section provides guidelines for managing financial claims related to contracts. By understanding these procedures, you can protect Canada's financial interests and ensure compliance with all relevant policies and directives. This includes handling claims for extra payments by contractors and managing financial claims owed to Canada.

structure_blocks.heading: Claims for extra payment
structure_blocks.text: Claims for extra payment

structure_blocks.heading: Claims for extra payment
structure_blocks.text: Contractors may occasionally submit claims for increased payments for firm price contracts due to changes caused by Canada. A firm price contract may not be amended for upward price revision without prior approval of Treasury Board (TB), unless the contract specifically includes an escalation clause, covering the adjustment requested or the contract conditions allowed for the requested adjustment.

structure_blocks.heading: Claims for extra payment
structure_blocks.text: TB approval is mandatory for all claims of extra payment, regardless of the amount involved. Typically, TB will not approve extra payment claims that result solely from:

structure_blocks.heading: Claims for extra payment
structure_blocks.text: labor or material cost increases

structure_blocks.heading: Claims for extra payment
structure_blocks.text: changes in freight rates

structure_blocks.heading: Claims for extra payment
structure_blocks.text: exchange rate revisions

structure_blocks.heading: Claims for extra payment
structure_blocks.text: contractor-caused delays

structure_blocks.heading: Claims for extra payment
structure_blocks.text: errors on the part of the contractor

structure_blocks.heading: Claims for extra payment
structure_blocks.text: other difficulties that the contractor overlooked, but should have foreseen

structure_blocks.heading: Claims for extra payment
structure_blocks.text: You should consult Legal Services to determine if the extra payment constitutes a contract amendment or an "ex gratia" payment.

structure_blocks.heading: Claims for extra payment
structure_blocks.text: The TB has granted full authority to deputy heads to make "ex gratia" payments and to designate officials within the department to act on their behalf. See the TB Directive on Payments.

structure_blocks.heading: Financial claims by Canada
structure_blocks.text: Financial claims by Canada

structure_blocks.heading: Financial claims by Canada
structure_blocks.text: When monies may be owing to Canada from contracting activities, it is essential to determine the liability and amount owing with assistance, as necessary from Legal Services.

structure_blocks.heading: Financial claims by Canada
structure_blocks.text: There are special procedures to be followed whenever there are suggestions of unreasonably high profits from any contract pursuant to the Defence Production Act or from any contract other than a competitive firm priced contract.

structure_blocks.heading: Financial claims by Canada
structure_blocks.text: There are two general categories of claims by Canada related to contracting activities:

structure_blocks.heading: Financial claims by Canada
structure_blocks.text: overpayments or overclaims identified through an audit

structure_blocks.heading: Financial claims by Canada
structure_blocks.text: legal disputes, including termination for default or bankruptcy when the amount owed is known and final

structure_blocks.heading: Financial claims by Canada
structure_blocks.text: Note: Either instance can only be determined when a contract has been completed or terminated.

structure_blocks.heading: Financial claims by Canada
structure_blocks.text: You should coordinate with the contractor to obtain concurrence on the final amount owing. After determining this amount, you must instruct the client to establish an accounts receivable. The client must notify you that it has been done and initiate collection efforts.

structure_blocks.heading: Financial claims by Canada
structure_blocks.text: The following roles contribute to managing these claims by Canada.

structure_blocks.heading: Buyers
structure_blocks.text: Buyers

structure_blocks.heading: Buyers
structure_blocks.text: You are responsible for reviewing the acceptability of the contractor claimed amount, responding to audit observations as required, liaising with the client for concurrence on the final amount, and advising the Cost and Profit Assurance Group (CPAG) of all settlements reached.

structure_blocks.heading: Assurance Services Group
structure_blocks.text: Assurance Services Group

structure_blocks.heading: Assurance Services Group
structure_blocks.text: The Assurance Services Group (ASG) provides contract related assurance and advisory services on credibility, integrity and reliability of financial and non-financial information used to support contractor claims.

structure_blocks.heading: Legal Services
structure_blocks.text: Legal Services

structure_blocks.heading: Legal Services
structure_blocks.text: Identifies risks and liabilities from claims faced by Canada as a result of claims against the contractor, notifies contractors when requested by the buyer of Canada’s claim, and initiates legal action against the contractor.

structure_blocks.heading: Industrial Technologies Office of Innovation, Science and Economic Development Canada (ISED)
heading_path: Industrial Technologies Office of Innovation, Science and Economic Development Canada (ISED)
structure_blocks.text: Industrial Technologies Office of Innovation, Science and Economic Development Canada (ISED)

structure_blocks.heading: Industrial Technologies Office of Innovation, Science and Economic Development Canada (ISED)
heading_path: Industrial Technologies Office of Innovation, Science and Economic Development Canada (ISED)
structure_blocks.text: Handles the follow-up of audit qualifications or observations raised by ASG , except for audit issues involving the interpretation of clauses related to contract cost principles, which are the responsibility of Public Services and Procurement Canada (PSPC). Additionally, resolves issues and disputes in joint Canadian Commercial Corporation and ISED agreements.

structure_blocks.heading: Recovery and settlement of contract claims
heading_path: Recovery and settlement of contract claims
structure_blocks.text: Recovery and settlement of contract claims

structure_blocks.heading: Recovery and settlement of contract claims
heading_path: Recovery and settlement of contract claims
structure_blocks.text: The recovery and settlement of contract claims adjustments can only be made if the contract, and its terms and conditions, contain clauses that allow for audits and for recovery.

structure_blocks.heading: Recovery and settlement of contract claims
heading_path: Recovery and settlement of contract claims
structure_blocks.text: This process addresses overclaims and excess profits as identified under the Cost and Profit Assurance Program (CPAP) which is delivered by the ASG within the Price Support Directorate (PSD) of the Procurement Support Services Sector (PSSS) of PSPC .

structure_blocks.heading: Recovery and settlement of contract claims
heading_path: Recovery and settlement of contract claims
structure_blocks.text: The CPAP uses account verification tools to demonstrate that the price charged to Canada aligns with the contract, safeguarding Canada from overpaying on contracts, primarily in regard of major procurements. Assurance engagements are undertaken as a result of either an assurance strategy developed with the assistance of ASG ’s assurance advisors, or as a response to risks identified by a buyer, price advisor, or other government stakeholders.

structure_blocks.heading: Assurance engagement
structure_blocks.text: Assurance engagement

structure_blocks.heading: Assurance engagement
structure_blocks.text: The process for the recovery and settlement of contract claims adjustments starts upon receipt of the assurance engagement report by the assurance advisor.

structure_blocks.heading: Assurance engagement
structure_blocks.text: Throughout the assurance engagement, all key stakeholders collaborate closely and maintain regular communication. This includes the assurance advisor, who provides guidance on assurance-related matters, the auditor team conducting the engagement, the PSPC buyer who manages the contract, the client and, when applicable, the price advisor, who was involved in negotiating the pricing arrangements for the contracts under examination.

structure_blocks.heading: Assurance engagement
structure_blocks.text: Auditors review claims against the terms of the contract and focus on:

structure_blocks.heading: Assurance engagement
structure_blocks.text: inadequate accounting and labour recording systems

structure_blocks.heading: Assurance engagement
structure_blocks.text: non-compliance with contract terms and conditions, Canada’s Contract Cost Principles 1031-2 and the Profit Principles (section 5.2 of the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing )

structure_blocks.heading: Assurance engagement
structure_blocks.text: billing errors resulting from incorrect rates, quantities or invoiced amounts

structure_blocks.heading: Assurance engagement
structure_blocks.text: profit variance in excess of authorized contract profit

structure_blocks.heading: Notification report and action plan
structure_blocks.text: Notification report and action plan

structure_blocks.heading: Notification report
structure_blocks.text: Notification report

structure_blocks.heading: Notification report
structure_blocks.text: A notification report is initiated, drafted and prepared by the ASG. assurance advisor. It is prepared to outline the findings and recommended actions in dealing with the contractor’s claims for payment or excess profit, its accounting practices, or it’s internal controls.

structure_blocks.heading: Notification report
structure_blocks.text: This report summarizes the results of the assurance engagement report and provides advice on the actions required by government representatives to mitigate the risk of overpayment on current and future contracts. The engagement report contains the full details of the assurance engagement results.

structure_blocks.heading: Notification report
structure_blocks.text: The notification report is intended to support buyers in the following areas:

structure_blocks.heading: Notification report
structure_blocks.text: negotiations with the offeror on final settlement of financial claims for payment

structure_blocks.heading: Notification report
structure_blocks.text: follow-up on matters of offeror compliance, as it relates to records, internal controls, cost accounting or billing practices

structure_blocks.heading: Notification report
structure_blocks.text: documentation and approval of the disposition of findings raised in the assurance engagement report

structure_blocks.heading: Notification report
structure_blocks.text: strategic decision-making, including modification to the basis of payment, clarifying terms and conditions of contracts, and modifications in costing methodology.

structure_blocks.heading: Notification report
structure_blocks.text: It includes:

structure_blocks.heading: Notification report
structure_blocks.text: a plain language description of proposed adjustments to claims for payment with details on its findings, time period and contract(s) covered by the engagement, and information on internal control or accounting system weaknesses

structure_blocks.heading: Notification report
structure_blocks.text: identification of the potential risks on current and future contracts awarded to the offeror based on issues identified in the audit

structure_blocks.heading: Notification report
structure_blocks.text: recommendations requiring response by the buyer

structure_blocks.heading: Notification report
structure_blocks.text: a request for an action plan in response to recommendations contained in the notification report with expected timelines for completion.

structure_blocks.heading: Action plan
structure_blocks.text: Action plan

structure_blocks.heading: Action plan
structure_blocks.text: The action plan is prepared and approved by procurement and responds to recommendations contained in the notification report, as prepared by ASG.

structure_blocks.heading: Action plan
structure_blocks.text: You, as the buyer, must provide the action plan to the assurance advisor within 30 calendar days of receipt of the notification report.

structure_blocks.heading: Action plan
structure_blocks.text: You should provide updates to the assurance advisor on progress made against the action plan on a monthly or as required basis.

structure_blocks.heading: Support and negotiation assistance
structure_blocks.text: Support and negotiation assistance

structure_blocks.heading: Support and negotiation assistance
structure_blocks.text: You will receive assistance from the assurance advisor, which may include advice on pursuing recoveries, recommendations for strengthening internal controls within the business accounting systems or suggestions for modifying the contract terms and conditions. Additionally, the assurance advisor will help clarify any questions and obtaining additional details from the auditor related to basis of support for adjustments to contractor claims and other relevant issues, as necessary.

structure_blocks.heading: Support and negotiation assistance
structure_blocks.text: The technical complexity of the auditor's findings may require support from a professional accountant to aid in negotiations. Requests for specialized assistance should be directed to ASG.

structure_blocks.heading: Support and negotiation assistance
structure_blocks.text: You must seek approval before rejecting any proposed adjustments to the contractors claims and before discussing them with the contractor. This must include the client, you as the PSPC buyer, and PSSS , as outlined in Approvals and disputes . You must obtain consensus from all before finalizing any rejection of claim adjustments.

structure_blocks.heading: Reporting, repayment, and closeout
structure_blocks.text: Reporting, repayment, and closeout

structure_blocks.heading: Reporting, repayment, and closeout
structure_blocks.text: ASG monitors and reports on recovery and settlement actions and will follow-up with you until all recommendations contained in the notification report are resolved. Semi-annually, ASG will report to procurement’s senior management on the status of recoveries, resolution of adjustments and lessons learned from the assurance engagement process.

structure_blocks.heading: Reporting, repayment, and closeout
structure_blocks.text: When you are provided a cheque to repay for overclaimed amounts or excess profits, you must provide the following to the assurance advisor within seven calendar days of deposit:

structure_blocks.heading: Reporting, repayment, and closeout
structure_blocks.text: copy of contractor’s cheque(s)

structure_blocks.heading: Reporting, repayment, and closeout
structure_blocks.text: copy of the official receipt from the client’s revenue and receivables cashier office

structure_blocks.heading: Reporting, repayment, and closeout
structure_blocks.text: copy of the client’s refund coding form indicating the financial general ledger account coding

structure_blocks.heading: Reporting, repayment, and closeout
structure_blocks.text: The process concludes with the administrative closeout. A report is prepared by the assurance advisor that summarizes the disposition of claim adjustments and matters related to contractor compliance for your concurrence. Closeout is finalized upon the report’s approval as outlined in Approvals and disputes .

structure_blocks.heading: Approvals and disputes
structure_blocks.text: Approvals and disputes

structure_blocks.heading: Approvals and disputes
structure_blocks.text: Sign-off thresholds with tiered delegations are required from all three parties namely the client, you as the PSPC buyer and PSSS . The management level required for sign-off is determined by the total dollar value of the contract claims adjustments, as stated in the notification report.

structure_blocks.heading: Approvals and disputes
structure_blocks.text: The sign-off thresholds are as follows:

structure_blocks.heading: Approvals and disputes
structure_blocks.text: senior directors and regional directors: up to $300,000

structure_blocks.heading: Approvals and disputes
structure_blocks.text: director generals: between $300,001 and $999,999

structure_blocks.heading: Approvals and disputes
structure_blocks.text: assistant deputy minister (ADM), Defence and Marine Procurement Branch or ADM , Procurement Branch (signoff will by the ADM under which the procurement was awarded): 1 million dollars and over

structure_blocks.heading: Approvals and disputes
structure_blocks.text: Disputes that can’t be solved amicably are referred to Legal Services for an assessment of the facts involved, including the strength of the contract language concerning audits and recoveries and with a listing of all steps involved that led the parties to the dispute.

raw_text:
Financial claims

This section provides guidelines for managing financial claims related to contracts. By understanding these procedures, you can protect Canada's financial interests and ensure compliance with all relevant policies and directives. This includes handling claims for extra payments by contractors and managing financial claims owed to Canada.

Claims for extra payment

Contractors may occasionally submit claims for increased payments for firm price contracts due to changes caused by Canada. A firm price contract may not be amended for upward price revision without prior approval of Treasury Board (TB), unless the contract specifically includes an escalation clause, covering the adjustment requested or the contract conditions allowed for the requested adjustment.

TB approval is mandatory for all claims of extra payment, regardless of the amount involved. Typically, TB will not approve extra payment claims that result solely from:

labor or material cost increases

changes in freight rates

exchange rate revisions

contractor-caused delays

errors on the part of the contractor

other difficulties that the contractor overlooked, but should have foreseen

You should consult Legal Services to determine if the extra payment constitutes a contract amendment or an "ex gratia" payment.

The TB has granted full authority to deputy heads to make "ex gratia" payments and to designate officials within the department to act on their behalf. See the TB Directive on Payments.

Financial claims by Canada

When monies may be owing to Canada from contracting activities, it is essential to determine the liability and amount owing with assistance, as necessary from Legal Services.

There are special procedures to be followed whenever there are suggestions of unreasonably high profits from any contract pursuant to the Defence Production Act or from any contract other than a competitive firm priced contract.

There are two general categories of claims by Canada related to contracting activities:

overpayments or overclaims identified through an audit

legal disputes, including termination for default or bankruptcy when the amount owed is known and final

Note: Either instance can only be determined when a contract has been completed or terminated.

You should coordinate with the contractor to obtain concurrence on the final amount owing. After determining this amount, you must instruct the client to establish an accounts receivable. The client must notify you that it has been done and initiate collection efforts.

The following roles contribute to managing these claims by Canada.

Buyers

You are responsible for reviewing the acceptability of the contractor claimed amount, responding to audit observations as required, liaising with the client for concurrence on the final amount, and advising the Cost and Profit Assurance Group (CPAG) of all settlements reached.

Assurance Services Group

The Assurance Services Group (ASG) provides contract related assurance and advisory services on credibility, integrity and reliability of financial and non-financial information used to support contractor claims.

Legal Services

Identifies risks and liabilities from claims faced by Canada as a result of claims against the contractor, notifies contractors when requested by the buyer of Canada’s claim, and initiates legal action against the contractor.

Industrial Technologies Office of Innovation, Science and Economic Development Canada (ISED)

Handles the follow-up of audit qualifications or observations raised by ASG , except for audit issues involving the interpretation of clauses related to contract cost principles, which are the responsibility of Public Services and Procurement Canada (PSPC). Additionally, resolves issues and disputes in joint Canadian Commercial Corporation and ISED agreements.

Recovery and settlement of contract claims

The recovery and settlement of contract claims adjustments can only be made if the contract, and its terms and conditions, contain clauses that allow for audits and for recovery.

This process addresses overclaims and excess profits as identified under the Cost and Profit Assurance Program (CPAP) which is delivered by the ASG within the Price Support Directorate (PSD) of the Procurement Support Services Sector (PSSS) of PSPC .

The CPAP uses account verification tools to demonstrate that the price charged to Canada aligns with the contract, safeguarding Canada from overpaying on contracts, primarily in regard of major procurements. Assurance engagements are undertaken as a result of either an assurance strategy developed with the assistance of ASG ’s assurance advisors, or as a response to risks identified by a buyer, price advisor, or other government stakeholders.

Assurance engagement

The process for the recovery and settlement of contract claims adjustments starts upon receipt of the assurance engagement report by the assurance advisor.

Throughout the assurance engagement, all key stakeholders collaborate closely and maintain regular communication. This includes the assurance advisor, who provides guidance on assurance-related matters, the auditor team conducting the engagement, the PSPC buyer who manages the contract, the client and, when applicable, the price advisor, who was involved in negotiating the pricing arrangements for the contracts under examination.

Auditors review claims against the terms of the contract and focus on:

inadequate accounting and labour recording systems

non-compliance with contract terms and conditions, Canada’s Contract Cost Principles 1031-2 and the Profit Principles (section 5.2 of the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing )

billing errors resulting from incorrect rates, quantities or invoiced amounts

profit variance in excess of authorized contract profit

Notification report and action plan

Notification report

A notification report is initiated, drafted and prepared by the ASG. assurance advisor. It is prepared to outline the findings and recommended actions in dealing with the contractor’s claims for payment or excess profit, its accounting practices, or it’s internal controls.

This report summarizes the results of the assurance engagement report and provides advice on the actions required by government representatives to mitigate the risk of overpayment on current and future contracts. The engagement report contains the full details of the assurance engagement results.

The notification report is intended to support buyers in the following areas:

negotiations with the offeror on final settlement of financial claims for payment

follow-up on matters of offeror compliance, as it relates to records, internal controls, cost accounting or billing practices

documentation and approval of the disposition of findings raised in the assurance engagement report

strategic decision-making, including modification to the basis of payment, clarifying terms and conditions of contracts, and modifications in costing methodology.

It includes:

a plain language description of proposed adjustments to claims for payment with details on its findings, time period and contract(s) covered by the engagement, and information on internal control or accounting system weaknesses

identification of the potential risks on current and future contracts awarded to the offeror based on issues identified in the audit

recommendations requiring response by the buyer

a request for an action plan in response to recommendations contained in the notification report with expected timelines for completion.

Action plan

The action plan is prepared and approved by procurement and responds to recommendations contained in the notification report, as prepared by ASG.

You, as the buyer, must provide the action plan to the assurance advisor within 30 calendar days of receipt of the notification report.

You should provide updates to the assurance advisor on progress made against the action plan on a monthly or as required basis.

Support and negotiation assistance

You will receive assistance from the assurance advisor, which may include advice on pursuing recoveries, recommendations for strengthening internal controls within the business accounting systems or suggestions for modifying the contract terms and conditions. Additionally, the assurance advisor will help clarify any questions and obtaining additional details from the auditor related to basis of support for adjustments to contractor claims and other relevant issues, as necessary.

The technical complexity of the auditor's findings may require support from a professional accountant to aid in negotiations. Requests for specialized assistance should be directed to ASG.

You must seek approval before rejecting any proposed adjustments to the contractors claims and before discussing them with the contractor. This must include the client, you as the PSPC buyer, and PSSS , as outlined in Approvals and disputes . You must obtain consensus from all before finalizing any rejection of claim adjustments.

Reporting, repayment, and closeout

ASG monitors and reports on recovery and settlement actions and will follow-up with you until all recommendations contained in the notification report are resolved. Semi-annually, ASG will report to procurement’s senior management on the status of recoveries, resolution of adjustments and lessons learned from the assurance engagement process.

When you are provided a cheque to repay for overclaimed amounts or excess profits, you must provide the following to the assurance advisor within seven calendar days of deposit:

copy of contractor’s cheque(s)

copy of the official receipt from the client’s revenue and receivables cashier office

copy of the client’s refund coding form indicating the financial general ledger account coding

The process concludes with the administrative closeout. A report is prepared by the assurance advisor that summarizes the disposition of claim adjustments and matters related to contractor compliance for your concurrence. Closeout is finalized upon the report’s approval as outlined in Approvals and disputes .

Approvals and disputes

Sign-off thresholds with tiered delegations are required from all three parties namely the client, you as the PSPC buyer and PSSS . The management level required for sign-off is determined by the total dollar value of the contract claims adjustments, as stated in the notification report.

The sign-off thresholds are as follows:

senior directors and regional directors: up to $300,000

director generals: between $300,001 and $999,999

assistant deputy minister (ADM), Defence and Marine Procurement Branch or ADM , Procurement Branch (signoff will by the ADM under which the procurement was awarded): 1 million dollars and over

Disputes that can’t be solved amicably are referred to Legal Services for an assessment of the facts involved, including the strength of the contract language concerning audits and recoveries and with a listing of all steps involved that led the parties to the dispute.

Financial claims

This section provides guidelines for managing financial claims related to contracts. By understanding these procedures, you can protect Canada's financial interests and ensure compliance with all relevant policies and directives. This includes handling claims for extra payments by contractors and managing financial claims owed to Canada.

Claims for extra payment

Contractors may occasionally submit claims for increased payments for firm price contracts due to changes caused by Canada. A firm price contract may not be amended for upward price revision without prior approval of Treasury Board (TB), unless the contract specifically includes an escalation clause, covering the adjustment requested or the contract conditions allowed for the requested adjustment.

TB approval is mandatory for all claims of extra payment, regardless of the amount involved. Typically, TB will not approve extra payment claims that result solely from:

labor or material cost increases

changes in freight rates

exchange rate revisions

contractor-caused delays

errors on the part of the contractor

other difficulties that the contractor overlooked, but should have foreseen

You should consult Legal Services to determine if the extra payment constitutes a contract amendment or an "ex gratia" payment.

The TB has granted full authority to deputy heads to make "ex gratia" payments and to designate officials within the department to act on their behalf. See the TB Directive on Payments.

billing errors resulting from incorrect rates, quantities or invoiced amounts

profit variance in excess of authorized contract profit

Notification report and action plan

A notification report is initiated, drafted and prepared by the ASG. assurance advisor. It is prepared to outline the findings and recommended actions in dealing with the contractor’s claims for payment or excess profit, its accounting practices, or it’s internal controls.

identification of the potential risks on current and future contracts awarded to the offeror based on issues identified in the audit

recommendations requiring response by the buyer

a request for an action plan in response to recommendations contained in the notification report with expected timelines for completion.

Additionally, the assurance advisor will help clarify any questions and obtaining additional details from the auditor related to basis of support for adjustments to contractor claims and other relevant issues, as necessary.

The technical complexity of the auditor's findings may require support from a professional accountant to aid in negotiations. Requests for specialized assistance should be directed to ASG.
